channel.title: Climate Risk (Google Alerts)
channel.description: Últimos artigos de Climate Risk (Google Alerts)

item.title: Empowering Makira's Frontline: Strengthening Capacity in Climate Health and Disaster Risk ...
item.description:
A total of 36 clinical and public health workers across Makira Province participated in the Pilot Training on Climate Change and Health and Disaster Risk Management to strengthen their capacity to protect communities from the increasing health challenges posed by extreme weather events, sea-level rise, and disease outbreaks.  

Through the training, health workers learned essential knowledge, practical tools, and hands-on exercises to develop local action plans and standard operating procedures for disaster preparedness and response. This is important after a baseline knowledge assessment conducted across seven health facilities, including Kirakira Hospital, identified key gaps in workforce knowledge and practice related to climate change and health and disaster risk management (DRM).

Makira Provincial Health Supervising Director, Dr Aloysius Samo, Jr. thanked WHO for providing technical support. Photo: WHO/Esther Nuria

Makira Provincial Health Supervising Director, Dr Aloysius Samo, Jr., expressed his appreciation to the Ministry of Health and Medical Services (MHMS), World Health Organization (WHO), Global Environment Facility (GEF), the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP), and the facilitators for bringing the pilot DRM training to Makira Ulawa Province. He said this pilot training is a first of its kind, especially for the provincial health sector. “Since more than 5 years serving the province, this is the first time I have witnessed and been part of an integrated training. I wanted to thank WHO for the technical support provided, and all the facilitators for their expertise shared during the training.”

He encouraged participants to make good use of the knowledge and skills obtained during the training. He noted that the knowledge and skills gained will save families and communities across Makira Province when there is a disaster.

The training, the second after Temotu last year, is made possible by GEF through UNDP. Photo: WHO/Esther Nuria

Director of the Environmental Health Division, Ms Ethel Mapolu, speaking on behalf of the Acting Permanent Secretary for Health and Medical Services, Dr Gregory Jilini, acknowledged and thanked the participants for attending the training. Ms Mapolu noted that Makira Ulawa Province is highly vulnerable to the impacts of climate change and disasters, with communities often experiencing the frontline impacts of cyclones, sea level rise, earthquakes, water insecurity, and disease outbreaks, among other climate-related challenges that often disrupt essential health services.

"We cannot ignore the risks posed by climate change and its impacts on health. Therefore, we must put measures in place to ensure that communities are adequately prepared and their well-being protected against the impact of a rapidly changing climate,” she said.

Mr Georgios Theocharopoulos, WHO Technical Officer for Health Emergencies. Photo: WHO/Esther Nuria

WHO Technical Officer for Health Emergencies Mr Georgios Theocharopoulos emphasized that the health impacts of the climate crisis are already being felt across Solomon Islands, with drought recently reported by some communities.

“Building resilient health systems that can withstand and respond to these events requires sustained and robust investment in our frontline workforce. By working together, Government, communities, and partners, our ability to protect the most vulnerable becomes stronger and more effective. This training is a step in that direction.” He further acknowledged the strong leadership of MHMS and thanked GEF and UNDP for their continuous support.

Organized by the MHMS – Environmental Health Division, in collaboration with WHO, the training is made possible by GEF through UNDP. This is the second pilot training conducted, the first being in Temotu last year.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Pandemic detour leads to clues about a mysterious disease | MSUToday
item.description:
When the COVID-19 pandemic shut down international travel in 2020, Michigan State University researcher Eric Benbow faced a problem.

A $2.5 million research project designed to study an environmental pathogen in South America was suddenly on hold. With fieldwork canceled and uncertainty surrounding when travel might resume, Benbow and his collaborators needed a new plan.

That unexpected detour led to a surprising discovery — and new insights into a disease that has puzzled scientists for decades.

In a study published in Communications Medicine, an international team of researchers examined the environmental and human factors that influence the distribution of Buruli ulcer, a neglected tropical disease caused by the bacterium Mycobacterium ulcerans. The work helps explain how ecosystems, climate, land use and human activities interact to shape disease risk.

“People often think about infectious diseases in terms of person-to-person transmission,” said Benbow, professor in the Department of Entomology in the College Agriculture and Natural Resources and in the Department of Osteopathic Medical Specialties in the College of Osteopathic Medicine. “But this pathogen lives in the environment. To understand the disease, we have to understand the ecosystem.”

Buruli ulcer is most common in parts of West and Central Africa and can cause severe skin ulcers, scarring and disability. Yet despite decades of study, scientists still do not fully understand how people become infected.

“It’s been called the ‘mysterious disease’ for decades because no one really knew how it was transmitted,” Benbow said.

Using hundreds of environmental variables, researchers identified patterns linking disease occurrence to factors such as flooding, land disturbance, agriculture, invasive plant species and climate conditions.

The findings support a growing body of research showing that environmental change can influence where pathogens persist and where people are most likely to encounter them.

“We’ve been taking a One Health approach to this disease for more than 15 years,” Benbow said. “You can’t separate human health from environmental health.”

The study also builds on an unexpected discovery made during the pandemic.

Unable to travel to South America, Benbow’s team shifted its attention to the southeastern United States after uncovering an old study suggesting the pathogen might exist there. Using newer molecular tools, the researchers sampled aquatic habitats across the region and confirmed the bacterium’s presence.

“We found it,” Benbow said.

The researchers later found other areas in the U.S. that are favorable and, therefore, were able to expand the known environmental range of the pathogen. Combined with the study’s global modeling results, the findings suggest that suitable environmental conditions for Mycobacterium ulcerans may exist in more places than previously recognized, even where human cases of Buruli ulcer have not been reported.

That discovery aligned with the new global modeling work, which identified portions of the southeastern United States as environmentally suitable for the pathogen, despite no known locally acquired human cases of Buruli ulcer.

The question now is why.

“Why are people getting the disease in some places and not others?” Benbow said. “That’s one of the major questions we’re trying to answer.”

While the study focuses on Buruli ulcer, Benbow says the broader implications extend far beyond a single disease.

“We’re using Buruli ulcer as a model system,” he said. “The larger question is how environmental change influences disease risk.”

Researchers increasingly recognize that human health is connected to the health of ecosystems. Changes such as agricultural expansion, invasive species, urban growth, mining and shifting climate conditions can alter where pathogens persist, how they disperse and how people encounter them.

By identifying the environmental conditions associated with disease-causing organisms, scientists can better anticipate future risks, improve surveillance efforts and help public health officials focus resources in areas where disease emergence may be more likely.

“The goal isn’t just to understand where a disease is today,” Benbow said. “It’s to understand the conditions that allow it to emerge so we can be better prepared for what’s coming next. These pathogens don’t suddenly appear out of nowhere. Many are already part of the natural environment. The challenge is understanding what conditions allow them to become a threat. When we alter landscapes, we can sometimes wake sleeping giants.”

The study included researchers from Michigan State University, Mississippi State University, the University of Montpellier and several international institutions.

item.title: How Lion reimagines employee journeys for an AI future | EY - Australia
item.description:
EY refers to the global organisation, and may refer to one or more, of the member firms of Ernst & Young Global Limited, each of which is a separate legal entity. Ernst & Young Global Limited, a UK company limited by guarantee, does not provide services to clients.

How EY can help

  • Our EY Operational Resilience team can help your business avoid catastrophic failure events and recover service delivery quickly.

    Read more

Lion is a US$2.5 billion beverage manufacturer operating across Australia, New Zealand and the United States, renowned for its iconic beer, spirits, wine and dairy brands. Lion’s complex manufacturing, distribution and retail channels require tight coordination to keep production running, ensure dependable deliveries and provide excellent customer experience.
 

Market dynamics have evolved in recent years as consumer preferences have shifted to non-alcoholic beverages. Changing demand in core categories made cost reduction a growing business imperative. With a long history of innovation in manufacturing and fostering a strong work culture for its 6,000 employees, Lion wanted to modernise its operations to create a leaner, more agile organisation while protecting frontline capacity and customer outcomes.
 

As Lion expanded, People and Culture support functions presented an opportunity to increase efficiency and reimagine employee experiences. The HR journey, from recruitment and onboarding to career development and retirement, relied on legacy tools and underutilised technology. Within this fragmented environment, HR recruiting and support teams faced challenges navigating multiple touchpoints and applications to complete routine tasks.

Lion identified workflow friction as a significant transformation opportunity. By streamlining workflows, simplifying the technology estate and improving visibility, the company could reduce process complexity. This transformation would not only elevate organisational culture and employee experience but also enhance frontline performance in alignment with its strategic value propositions.
 

The opportunity for transformation extended into Lion’s IT and operational landscape. The company’s IT support operated on Cherwell software, and they offered the scope to enhance workflow maturity and visibility. By relying on a mix of tools to manage established cybersecurity processes, the organisation created an opportunity to develop more purpose built risk management capabilities. Additionally, expanding Operational Technology (OT) and Configuration Management Database (CMDB) coverage across manufacturing promised to enhance operational oversight and incident response.
 

Lion’s senior leadership asked a practical question when exploring the possibilities: How can the company manage costs and transform efficiency while safeguarding operational resilience, innovation and a strong employee culture?

Rather than seeking immediate, one-off savings, Lion recognised the necessity for a comprehensive transformation – one that would involve redesigning workflows and leveraging existing technology investments to generate greater value across key areas, including HR production, logistics and IT.

To drive this transformation, Lion engaged Ernst & Young (EY) professionals to help transform its ServiceNow technology into a scalable, people-centered platform and accelerate AI-enabled automation to deliver efficiency gains, stellar employee experiences and a flexible foundation for continuous improvement.

item.title: New Zealand climate goals at risk as emissions progress stalls, report says - KFGO
item.description:
WELLINGTON, July 22 (Reuters) – New Zealand’s greenhouse gas emissions are falling too slowly to meet national climate targets, with progress stalling in 2024 and key ​goals for the next decade now at heightened ‌risk, the Climate Change Commission said on Wednesday.

In its annual emissions monitoring report, the independent advisory body said the pace of emissions cuts would need to more than double over the next few ‌years ​if the country was to get ⁠back on track.

“This is ⁠a clear warning sign,” commission Chief Executive Jo Hendy said. “Emissions are gradually falling but progress stalled in 2024, and current policy settings are not delivering at the ​pace needed.”

The report said government decisions over the next 12 to 24 months would be critical. It found ⁠New Zealand’s second and third ⁠emissions budgets were at high risk, while ​the 2030 target for biogenic methane — largely produced by livestock — was ​unlikely to be met.

New Zealand has legislated emissions ‌budgets as part of its plan to reach net zero long-lived greenhouse gas emissions by 2050, while also reducing methane from agriculture and waste.

The commission said faster uptake ⁠of existing low-emissions technologies could reduce costs for households and businesses, as well as cut exposure to volatile fossil fuel prices. ⁠It cited electric ‌vehicles, rooftop solar, batteries and industrial heat ⁠pumps as technologies that were already cheaper ​over ‌time in some common uses.

Hendy said barriers ​such as ⁠upfront costs were slowing adoption, even where long-term savings were available.

The commission urged the government to use targeted finance, clearer investment signals and better information to help households and businesses switch away from fossil fuels.

(Reporting by Lucy Craymer; Editing ​by Chris Reese)
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Cropin & Google Cloud: Tackling Climate Risks in Procurement
item.description:
Cropin & Google Cloud: Tackling Climate Risks in Procurement

Cropin and Google Cloud have launched OrbitAI, an AI platform built to help food and beverage procurement teams manage supply chain risks from climate disruption. The system uses autonomous agents to monitor crop health and predict yield shocks across global sourcing zones.

Procurement managers in the food industry face mounting pressure from volatile weather patterns and shifting geopolitical conditions. OrbitAI could allow teams to identify potential shortages before they affect downstream operations, according to the companies.

The platform combines Cropin's agritech data with Google Cloud's enterprise infrastructure. It leverages Google Cloud's Gemini models to process planetary and agricultural data at scale.

Autonomous agents monitor supply risk

OrbitAI deploys specialised AI agents that operate independently to track crop health anomalies and predict regional yield shocks. The system alerts procurement managers to potential supply issues ahead of harvest cycles.

"OrbitAI represents a milestone in agricultural technology," says Krishna Kumar, Chief Executive Officer at Cropin. "The platform empowers businesses to secure their supply networks autonomously."

Food companies can use the platform to optimise sourcing strategies across different regions. This could enable procurement teams to navigate climate uncertainties with greater precision.

  • Track crop health anomalies across global sourcing zones.
  • Predict potential supply chain disruptions before harvest.
  • Optimise procurement decisions using autonomous AI agents.

Moving beyond cost to capacity

Sashikumar Sreedharan, Managing Director of Google Cloud India, explains that the industry is experiencing a shift in how automation delivers value for procurement operations.

Sashikumar says: "The next transformation in AI is moving away from technology demonstrations and focusing on measurable outcomes, specifically addressing the core challenge of 'cost to serve' versus 'capacity to serve'."

"By building on Google Cloud's AI infrastructure, OrbitAI allows organisations to deploy agentic workflows that scale capacity exponentially, tackling complex real-world challenges with unprecedented pace and efficiency."

Google Cloud provides the computational power and security required for large-scale enterprise deployments. The system can handle massive datasets across diverse geographies.

Climate shocks hit raw material flows

Climate change continues to disrupt the flow of raw materials to manufacturing facilities. The El Niño weather cycle induced severe droughts and shifting rainfall globally, according to recent weather reports.

Consecutive heatwaves in May and June 2026 cut UK milk deliveries by nearly four% compared to weekly averages. These disruptions demonstrate the volatility procurement teams must now manage.

Cropin provides real-time visibility into the lowest tiers of the agricultural supply chain. The platform helps companies reduce waste by aligning agricultural production directly with processing schedules.

This capability could ensure that food processors maintain consistent production levels as procurement teams gain advance warning of supply gaps that might require alternative sourcing arrangements.

We are helping organisations build more sustainable and predictable supply chains.

Global scalability for procurement frameworks

The platform is designed to scale from smallholder farms to massive commercial operations. This flexibility could allow multinational corporations to standardise their risk management frameworks on a global scale.

"Our partnership with Cropin brings scalable innovation to the agricultural ecosystem," says Yossi Matias, VP Engineering & Research at Google. "We are helping organisations build more sustainable and predictable supply chains."

As regulatory demands around traceability tighten, the platform also aims to assist in tracking environmental metrics. It provides the data needed to verify sustainable sourcing practices.

The system offers procurement teams a way to document supply chain conditions throughout the growing cycle. This could support compliance with emerging regulations on environmental disclosure and supply chain transparency.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado
